   474→
   475→<AppOnly>
   476→
   477→## `runtime` configuration (Breaking change)
   478→
   479→The `runtime` [segment configuration](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/route-segment-config/runtime) previously supported a value of `experimental-edge` in addition to `edge`. Both configurations refer to the same thing, and to simplify the options, we will now error if `experimental-edge` is used. To fix this, update your `runtime` configuration to `edge`. A [codemod](/docs/app/guides/upgrading/codemods#app-dir-runtime-config-experimental-edge) is available to automatically do this.
   480→
   481→</AppOnly>
   482→
   483→## `fetch` requests
   484→
   485→[`fetch` requests](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/fetch) are no longer cached by default.
   486→
   487→To opt specific `fetch` requests into caching, you can pass the `cache: 'force-cache'` option.
   488→
   489→```js filename="app/layout.js"
   490→export default async function RootLayout() {
   491→  const a = await fetch('https://...') // Not Cached
   492→  const b = await fetch('https://...', { cache: 'force-cache' }) // Cached
   493→
   494→  // ...
   495→}
   496→```
   497→
   498→To opt all `fetch` requests in a layout or page into caching, you can use the `export const fetchCache = 'default-cache'` [segment config option](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/route-segment-config). If individual `fetch` requests specify a `cache` option, that will be used instead.
   499→
   500→```js filename="app/layout.js"
   501→// Since this is the root layout, all fetch requests in the app
   502→// that don't set their own cache option will be cached.
   503→export const fetchCache = 'default-cache'
   504→
   505→export default async function RootLayout() {
   506→  const a = await fetch('https://...') // Cached
   507→  const b = await fetch('https://...', { cache: 'no-store' }) // Not cached
   508→
   509→  // ...
   510→}
   511→```
   512→
   513→## Route Handlers
   514→
   515→`GET` functions in [Route Handlers](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/route) are no longer cached by default. To opt `GET` methods into caching, you can use a [route config option](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/route-segment-config) such as `export const dynamic = 'force-static'` in your Route Handler file.
   516→
   517→```js filename="app/api/route.js"
   518→export const dynamic = 'force-static'
   519→
   520→export async function GET() {}
   521→```
   522→
   523→## Client Cache
   524→
   525→When navigating between pages via `<Link>` or `useRouter`, [page](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/page) segments are no longer reused from the [Client Cache](/docs/app/glossary#client-cache). However, they are still reused during browser backward and forward navigation and for shared layouts.
   526→
   527→To opt page segments into caching, you can use the [`staleTimes`](/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/staleTimes) config option:
   528→
   529→```js filename="next.config.js"
   530→/** @type {import('next').NextConfig} */
   531→const nextConfig = {
   532→  experimental: {
   533→    staleTimes: {
   534→      dynamic: 30,
   535→      static: 180,
   536→    },
   537→  },
   538→}
   539→
   540→module.exports = nextConfig
   541→```
   542→
   543→[Layouts](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/layout) and [loading states](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/loading) are still cached and reused on navigation.
   544→
   545→## `next/font`
   546→
   547→The `@next/font` package has been removed in favor of the built-in [`next/font`](/docs/app/api-reference/components/font). A [codemod is available](/docs/app/guides/upgrading/codemods#built-in-next-font) to safely and automatically rename your imports.
   548→
   549→```js filename="app/layout.js"
   550→// Before
   551→import { Inter } from '@next/font/google'
   552→
   553→// After
   554→import { Inter } from 'next/font/google'
   555→```
   556→
   557→## bundlePagesRouterDependencies
   558→
   559→`experimental.bundlePagesExternals` is now stable and renamed to `bundlePagesRouterDependencies`.
   560→
   561→```js filename="next.config.js"
   562→/** @type {import('next').NextConfig} */
   563→const nextConfig = {
   564→  // Before
   565→  experimental: {
   566→    bundlePagesExternals: true,
   567→  },
   568→
   569→  // After
   570→  bundlePagesRouterDependencies: true,
   571→}
   572→
   573→module.exports = nextConfig
   574→```
   575→
   576→## serverExternalPackages
   577→
   578→`experimental.serverComponentsExternalPackages` is now stable and renamed to `serverExternalPackages`.
   579→
   580→```js filename="next.config.js"
   581→/** @type {import('next').NextConfig} */
   582→const nextConfig = {
   583→  // Before
   584→  experimental: {
   585→    serverComponentsExternalPackages: ['package-name'],
   586→  },
   587→
   588→  // After
   589→  serverExternalPackages: ['package-name'],
   590→}
   591→
   592→module.exports = nextConfig
   593→```
   594→
   595→## Speed Insights
   596→
   597→Auto instrumentation for Speed Insights was removed in Next.js 15.
   598→
   599→To continue using Speed Insights, follow the [Vercel Speed Insights Quickstart](https://vercel.com/docs/speed-insights/quickstart) guide.
   600→
   601→## `NextRequest` Geolocation
   602→
   603→The `geo` and `ip` properties on `NextRequest` have been removed as these values are provided by your hosting provider. A [codemod](/docs/app/guides/upgrading/codemods#150) is available to automate this migration.
   604→
   605→If you are using Vercel, you can alternatively use the `geolocation` and `ipAddress` functions from [`@vercel/functions`](https://vercel.com/docs/functions/vercel-functions-package) instead:
   606→
   607→```ts filename="middleware.ts"
   608→import { geolocation } from '@vercel/functions'
   609→import type { NextRequest } from 'next/server'
   610→
   611→export function middleware(request: NextRequest) {
   612→  const { city } = geolocation(request)
   613→
   614→  // ...
   615→}
   616→```
   617→
   618→```ts filename="middleware.ts"
   619→import { ipAddress } from '@vercel/functions'
   620→import type { NextRequest } from 'next/server'
   621→
   622→export function middleware(request: NextRequest) {
   623→  const ip = ipAddress(request)
   624→
   625→  // ...
   626→}
   627→```
   628→

1→--- 2→title: How to upgrade to version 16 3→nav_title: Version 16 4→description: Upgrade your Next.js Application from Version 15 to 16. 5→--- 6→ 7→{/* The content of this doc is shared between the app and pages router. You can use the `Content` component to add content that is specific to the Pages Router. Any shared content should not be wrapped in a component. */} 8→ 9→## Upgrading from 15 to 16 10→ 11→### Using AI Agents with Next.js DevTools MCP 12→ 13→If you're using an AI coding assistant that supports the [Model Context Protocol (MCP)](https://modelcontextprotocol.io), you can use the **Next.js DevTools MCP** to automate the upgrade process and migration tasks. 14→ 15→#### Setup 16→ 17→Add the following configuration to your MCP client, for each coding agent you can read [this section](https://github.com/vercel/next-devtools-mcp#mcp-client-configuration) for configuration details. 18→ 19→**example:** 20→ 21→```json filename=".mcp.json" 22→{ 23→ "mcpServers": { 24→ "next-devtools": { 25→ "command": "npx", 26→ "args": ["-y", "next-devtools-mcp@latest"] 27→ } 28→ } 29→} 30→``` 31→ 32→For more information, visit the [`next-devtools-mcp`](https://github.com/vercel/next-devtools-mcp) documentation to configure with your MCP client. 33→ 34→> **Note:** Using `next-devtools-mcp@latest` ensures that your MCP client will always use the latest version of the Next.js DevTools MCP server. 35→ 36→#### Example Prompts 37→ 38→Once configured, you can use natural language prompts to upgrade your Next.js app: 39→ 40→**To upgrade to Next.js 16:** 41→ 42→Connect to your coding agent and then prompt: 43→ 44→```txt 45→Next Devtools, help me upgrade my Next.js app to version 16 46→``` 47→ 48→**To migrate to Cache Components (after upgrading to v16):** 49→ 50→Connect to your coding agent and then prompt: 51→ 52→```txt 53→Next Devtools, migrate my Next.js app to cache components 54→``` 55→ 56→Learn more in the documentation [here](/docs/app/guides/mcp). 57→ 58→### Using the Codemod 59→ 60→To update to Next.js version 16, you can use the `upgrade` [codemod](/docs/app/guides/upgrading/codemods#160): 61→ 62→```bash package="pnpm" 63→pnpm dlx @next/codemod@canary upgrade latest 64→``` 65→ 66→```bash package="npm" 67→npx @next/codemod@canary upgrade latest 68→``` 69→ 70→```bash package="yarn" 71→yarn dlx @next/codemod@canary upgrade latest 72→``` 73→ 74→```bash package="bun" 75→bunx @next/codemod@canary upgrade latest 76→``` 77→ 78→The [codemod](/docs/app/guides/upgrading/codemods#160) is able to: 79→ 80→- Update `next.config.js` to use the new `turbopack` configuration 81→- Migrate from `next lint` to the ESLint CLI 82→- Migrate from deprecated `middleware` convention to `proxy` 83→- Remove `unstable_` prefix from stabilized APIs 84→- Remove `experimental_ppr` Route Segment Config from pages and layouts 85→ 86→If you prefer to do it manually, install the latest Next.js and React versions: 87→ 88→```bash package="pnpm" 89→pnpm add next@latest react@latest react-dom@latest 90→``` 91→ 92→```bash package="npm" 93→npm install next@latest react@latest react-dom@latest 94→``` 95→ 96→```bash package="yarn" 97→yarn add next@latest react@latest react-dom@latest 98→``` 99→ 100→```bash package="bun" 101→bun add next@latest react@latest react-dom@latest 102→``` 103→ 104→If you are using TypeScript, ensure you also upgrade `@types/react` and `@types/react-dom` to their latest versions. 105→ 106→## Node.js runtime and browser support 107→ 108→| Requirement | Change / Details | 109→| ------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------ | 110→| Node.js 20.9+ | Minimum version now `20.9.0` (LTS); Node.js 18 no longer supported | 111→| TypeScript 5+ | Minimum version now `5.1.0` | 112→| Browsers | Chrome 111+, Edge 111+, Firefox 111+, Safari 16.4+ | 113→ 114→## Turbopack by default 115→ 116→Starting with **Next.js 16**, Turbopack is stable and used by default with `next dev` and `next build` 117→ 118→Previously you had to enable Turbopack using `--turbopack`, or `--turbo`. 119→ 120→```json filename="package.json" 121→{ 122→ "scripts": { 123→ "dev": "next dev --turbopack", 124→ "build": "next build --turbopack", 125→ "start": "next start" 126→ } 127→} 128→``` 129→ 130→This is no longer necessary. You can update your `package.json` scripts: 131→ 132→```json filename="package.json" 133→{ 134→ "scripts": { 135→ "dev": "next dev", 136→ "build": "next build", 137→ "start": "next start" 138→ } 139→} 140→``` 141→ 142→If your project has a [custom `webpack`](/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/webpack) configuration and you run `next build` (which now uses Turbopack by default), the build will **fail** to prevent misconfiguration issues. 143→ 144→You have a few different ways to address this: 145→ 146→- **Use Turbopack anyway:** Run with `next build --turbopack` to build using Turbopack and ignore your `webpack` config. 147→- **Switch to Turbopack fully:** Migrate your `webpack` config to Turbopack-compatible options. 148→- **Keep using Webpack:** Use the `--webpack` flag to opt out of Turbopack and build with Webpack. 149→ 150→> **Good to know**: If you see failing builds because a `webpack` configuration was found, but you don't define one yourself, it is likely that a plugin is adding a `webpack` option 151→ 152→### Opting out of Turbopack 153→ 154→If you need to continue using Webpack, you can opt out with the `--webpack` flag. For example, to use Turbopack in development but Webpack for production builds: 155→ 156→```json filename="package.json" 157→{ 158→ "scripts": { 159→ "dev": "next dev", 160→ "build": "next build --webpack", 161→ "start": "next start" 162→ } 163→} 164→``` 165→ 166→We recommend using Turbopack for development and production. Submit a comment to this [thread](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/discussions/77721), if you are unable to switch to Turbopack. 167→ 168→### Turbopack configuration location 169→ 170→The `experimental.turbopack` configuration is out of experimental. 171→ 172→```ts filename="next.config.ts" 173→import type { NextConfig } from 'next' 174→ 175→// Next.js 15 - experimental.turbopack 176→const nextConfig: NextConfig = { 177→ experimental: { 178→ turbopack: { 179→ // options 180→ }, 181→ }, 182→} 183→ 184→export default nextConfig 185→``` 186→ 187→You can use it as a top-level `turbopack` option: 188→ 189→```ts filename="next.config.ts" 190→import type { NextConfig } from 'next' 191→ 192→// Next.js 16 - turbopack at the top level of nextConfig 193→const nextConfig: NextConfig = { 194→ turbopack: { 195→ // options 196→ }, 197→} 198→ 199→export default nextConfig 200→``` 201→ 202→Make sure to review the `Turbopack` configuration [options](/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/turbopack). **Next.js 16** introduces various improvements and new options, for example: 203→ 204→- [Advanced Webpack loader conditions](/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/turbopack#advanced-webpack-loader-conditions) 205→- [debugIds](/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/turbopack#debug-ids) 206→ 207→### Resolve alias fallback 208→ 209→In some projects, client-side code may import files containing Node.js native modules. This will cause `Module not found: Can't resolve 'fs'` type of errors. 210→ 211→When this happens, you should refactor your code so that your client-side bundles do not reference these Node.js native modules. 212→ 213→However, in some cases, this might not be possible. In Webpack the `resolve.fallback` option was typically used to **silence** the error. Turbopack offers a similar option, using `turbopack.resolveAlias`. In this case, tell Turbopack to load an empty module when `fs` is requested for the browser. 214→ 215→```ts filename="next.config.ts" 216→import type { NextConfig } from 'next' 217→ 218→const nextConfig: NextConfig = { 219→ turbopack: { 220→ resolveAlias: { 221→ fs: { 222→ browser: './empty.ts', // We recommend to fix code imports before using this method 223→ }, 224→ }, 225→ }, 226→} 227→ 228→export default nextConfig 229→``` 230→ 231→It is preferable to refactor your modules so that client code doesn't ever import from modules using Node.js native modules. 232→ 233→### Sass node_modules imports 234→ 235→Turbopack fully supports importing Sass files from `node_modules`. Note that while Webpack allowed the legacy tilde (`~`) prefix, Turbopack does not support this syntax. 236→ 237→In Webpack: 238→ 239→```scss filename="styles/globals.scss" 240→@import '~bootstrap/dist/css/bootstrap.min.css'; 241→``` 242→ 243→In Turbopack: 244→ 245→```scss filename="styles/globals.scss" 246→@import 'bootstrap/dist/css/bootstrap.min.css'; 247→``` 248→ 249→If changing the imports is not possible, you can use `turbopack.resolveAlias`. For example: 250→ 251→```ts filename="next.config.ts" 252→import type { NextConfig } from 'next' 253→ 254→const nextConfig: NextConfig = { 255→ turbopack: { 256→ resolveAlias: { 257→ '~*': '*', 258→ }, 259→ }, 260→} 261→ 262→export default nextConfig 263→``` 264→ 265→### Turbopack File System Caching (beta) 266→ 267→Turbopack now supports filesystem caching in development, storing compiler artifacts on disk between runs for significantly faster compile times across restarts. 268→ 269→Enable filesystem caching in your configuration: 270→ 271→```ts filename="next.config.ts" switcher 272→import type { NextConfig } from 'next' 273→ 274→const nextConfig: NextConfig = { 275→ experimental: { 276→ turbopackFileSystemCacheForDev: true, 277→ }, 278→} 279→ 280→export default nextConfig 281→``` 282→ 283→```js filename="next.config.js" switcher 284→/** @type {import('next').NextConfig} */ 285→const nextConfig = { 286→ experimental: { 287→ turbopackFileSystemCacheForDev: true, 288→ }, 289→} 290→ 291→module.exports = nextConfig 292→``` 293→ 294→## Async Request APIs (Breaking change) 295→ 296→Version 15 introduced [Async Request APIs](https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-15#async-request-apis-breaking-change) as a breaking change, with **temporary** synchronous compatibility. 297→ 298→Starting with **Next.js 16**, synchronous access is fully removed. These APIs can only be accessed asynchronously. 299→ 300→- [`cookies`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/cookies) 301→- [`headers`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/headers) 302→- [`draftMode`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/draft-mode) 303→- `params` in [`layout.js`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/layout), [`page.js`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/page), [`route.js`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/route), [`default.js`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/default), [`opengraph-image`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/metadata/opengraph-image#opengraph-image), [`twitter-image`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/metadata/opengraph-image#twitter-image), [`icon`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/metadata/app-icons#icon), and [`apple-icon`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/metadata/app-icons#apple-icon). 304→- `searchParams` in [`page.js`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/page) 305→ 306→Use the [codemod](/docs/app/guides/upgrading/codemods#migrate-to-async-dynamic-apis) to migrate to async Request-time APIs. 307→ 308→### Migrating types for async Request-time APIs 309→ 310→To help migrate to async `params` and `searchParams`, you can run [`npx next typegen`](/docs/app/api-reference/cli/next#next-typegen-options) to automatically generate these globally available types helpers: 311→ 312→- [`PageProps`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/page#page-props-helper) 313→- [`LayoutProps`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/layout#layout-props-helper) 314→- [`RouteContext`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/route#route-context-helper) 315→ 316→> **Good to know**: `typegen` was introduced in Next.js 15.5 317→ 318→This simplifies type-safe migration to the new async API pattern, and enables you to update your components with full type safety, for example: 319→ 320→```tsx filename="/app/blog/[slug]/page.tsx" 321→export default async function Page(props: PageProps<'/blog/[slug]'>) { 322→ const { slug } = await props.params 323→ const query = await props.searchParams 324→ return

Blog Post: {slug}

325→} 326→``` 327→ 328→This approach gives you fully type-safe access to `props.params`, including the `slug`, and to `searchParams`, directly within your page. 329→ 330→## Async parameters for icon, and open-graph Image (Breaking change) 331→ 332→> The props passed to the image generating functions in `opengraph-image`, `twitter-image`, `icon`, and `apple-icon`, are now Promises. 333→ 334→In previous versions, both the `Image` (image generation function), and the `generateImageMetadata` received a `params` object. The `id` returned by `generateImageMetadata` was passed as a string to the image generation function. 335→ 336→```js filename="app/shop/[slug]/opengraph-image.js" 337→// Next.js 15 - synchronous params access 338→export function generateImageMetadata({ params }) { 339→ const { slug } = params 340→ return [{ id: '1' }, { id: '2' }] 341→} 342→ 343→// Next.js 15 - synchronous params and id access 344→export default function Image({ params, id }) { 345→ const slug = params.slug 346→ const imageId = id // string 347→ // ... 348→} 349→``` 350→ 351→Starting with **Next.js 16**, to align with the [Async Request APIs](#async-request-apis-breaking-change) change, the image generating function now receives `params` and `id` as promises. The `generateImageMetadata` function continues to receive synchronous `params`. 352→ 353→```js filename="app/shop/[slug]/opengraph-image.js" 354→export async function generateImageMetadata({ params }) { 355→ const { slug } = params 356→ return [{ id: '1' }, { id: '2' }] 357→} 358→ 359→// Next.js 16 - asynchronous params and id access 360→export default async function Image({ params, id }) { 361→ const { slug } = await params // params now async 362→ const imageId = await id // id is now Promise when using generateImageMetadata 363→ // ... 364→} 365→``` 366→ 367→## Async `id` parameter for `sitemap` (Breaking change) 368→ 369→Previously, the `id` values returned from [`generateSitemaps`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/generate-sitemaps) were passed directly to the `sitemap` generating function. 370→ 371→```js filename="app/product/sitemap.js" 372→export async function generateSitemaps() { 373→ return [{ id: 0 }, { id: 1 }, { id: 2 }, { id: 3 }] 374→} 375→ 376→// Next.js 15 - synchronous id access 377→export default async function sitemap({ id }) { 378→ const start = id * 50000 // id is a number 379→ // ... 380→} 381→``` 382→ 383→Starting with **Next.js 16**, the `sitemap` generating function now receives `id` as a promise. 384→ 385→```js filename="app/product/sitemap.js" 386→export async function generateSitemaps() { 387→ return [{ id: 0 }, { id: 1 }, { id: 2 }, { id: 3 }] 388→} 389→ 390→// Next.js 16 - asynchronous id access 391→export default async function sitemap({ id }) { 392→ const resolvedId = await id // id is now Promise 393→ const start = Number(resolvedId) * 50000 394→ // ... 395→} 396→``` 397→ 398→## React 19.2 399→ 400→The App Router in **Next.js 16** uses the latest React [Canary release](https://react.dev/blog/2023/05/03/react-canaries), which includes the newly released React 19.2 features and other features being incrementally stabilized. Highlights include: 401→ 402→- **[View Transitions](https://react.dev/reference/react/ViewTransition)**: Animate elements that update inside a Transition or navigation 403→- **[`useEffectEvent`](https://react.dev/reference/react/useEffectEvent)**: Extract non-reactive logic from Effects into reusable Effect Event functions 404→- **[Activity](https://react.dev/reference/react/Activity)**: Render "background activity" by hiding UI with `display: none` while maintaining state and cleaning up Effects 405→ 406→Learn more in the [React 19.2 announcement](https://react.dev/blog/2025/10/01/react-19-2). 407→ 408→## React Compiler Support 409→ 410→Built-in support for the React Compiler is now stable in **Next.js 16** following the React Compiler's 1.0 release. The React Compiler automatically memoizes components, reducing unnecessary re-renders with zero manual code changes. 411→ 412→The `reactCompiler` configuration option has been promoted from `experimental` to stable. It is not enabled by default as we continue gathering build performance data across different application types. 413→ 414→```ts filename="next.config.ts" switcher 415→import type { NextConfig } from 'next' 416→ 417→const nextConfig: NextConfig = { 418→ reactCompiler: true, 419→} 420→ 421→export default nextConfig 422→``` 423→ 424→```js filename="next.config.js" switcher 425→/** @type {import('next').NextConfig} */ 426→const nextConfig = { 427→ reactCompiler: true, 428→} 429→ 430→module.exports = nextConfig 431→``` 432→ 433→Install the latest version of the React Compiler plugin: 434→ 435→```bash package="pnpm" 436→pnpm add -D babel-plugin-react-compiler 437→``` 438→ 439→```bash package="npm" 440→npm install -D babel-plugin-react-compiler 441→``` 442→ 443→```bash package="yarn" 444→yarn add -D babel-plugin-react-compiler 445→``` 446→ 447→```bash package="bun" 448→bun add -D babel-plugin-react-compiler 449→``` 450→ 451→> **Good to know:** Expect compile times in development and during builds to be higher when enabling this option as the React Compiler relies on Babel. 452→ 453→## Caching APIs 454→ 455→### revalidateTag 456→ 457→[`revalidateTag`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/revalidateTag) has a new function signature. You can pass a [`cacheLife`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/cacheLife#reference) profile as the second argument. 458→ 459→```ts filename="app/actions.ts" switcher 460→'use server' 461→ 462→import { revalidateTag } from 'next/cache' 463→ 464→export async function updateArticle(articleId: string) { 465→ // Mark article data as stale - article readers see stale data while it revalidates 466→ revalidateTag(`article-${articleId}`, 'max') 467→} 468→``` 469→ 470→```js filename="app/actions.js" switcher 471→'use server' 472→ 473→import { revalidateTag } from 'next/cache' 474→ 475→export async function updateArticle(articleId) { 476→ // Mark article data as stale - article readers see stale data while it revalidates 477→ revalidateTag(`article-${articleId}`, 'max') 478→} 479→``` 480→ 481→Use `revalidateTag` for content where a slight delay in updates is acceptable, such as blog posts, product catalogs, or documentation. Users receive stale content while fresh data loads in the background. 482→ 483→### updateTag 484→ 485→[`updateTag`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/updateTag) is a new [Server Actions](/docs/app/getting-started/mutating-data#what-are-server-functions)-only API that provides **read-your-writes** semantics, where a user makes a change and the UI immediately shows the change, rather than stale data. 486→ 487→It does this by expiring and immediately refreshing data within the same request. 488→ 489→```ts filename="app/actions.ts" switcher 490→'use server' 491→ 492→import { updateTag } from 'next/cache' 493→ 494→export async function updateUserProfile(userId: string, profile: Profile) { 495→ await db.users.update(userId, profile) 496→ 497→ // Expire cache and refresh immediately - user sees their changes right away 498→ updateTag(`user-${userId}`) 499→} 500→``` 501→ 502→```js filename="app/actions.js" switcher 503→'use server' 504→ 505→import { updateTag } from 'next/cache' 506→ 507→export async function updateUserProfile(userId, profile) { 508→ await db.users.update(userId, profile) 509→ 510→ // Expire cache and refresh immediately - user sees their changes right away 511→ updateTag(`user-${userId}`) 512→} 513→``` 514→ 515→This ensures interactive features reflect changes immediately. Perfect for forms, user settings, and any workflow where users expect to see their updates instantly. 516→ 517→Learn more about when to use `updateTag` or `revalidateTag` [here](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/updateTag#when-to-use-updatetag). 518→ 519→### refresh 520→ 521→[`refresh`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/refresh) allows you to refresh the client router from within a Server Action. 522→ 523→```ts filename="app/actions.ts" switcher 524→'use server' 525→ 526→import { refresh } from 'next/cache' 527→ 528→export async function markNotificationAsRead(notificationId: string) { 529→ // Update the notification in the database 530→ await db.notifications.markAsRead(notificationId) 531→ 532→ // Refresh the notification count displayed in the header 533→ refresh() 534→} 535→``` 536→ 537→```js filename="app/actions.js" switcher 538→'use server' 539→ 540→import { refresh } from 'next/cache' 541→ 542→export async function markNotificationAsRead(notificationId) { 543→ // Update the notification in the database 544→ await db.notifications.markAsRead(notificationId) 545→ 546→ // Refresh the notification count displayed in the header 547→ refresh() 548→} 549→``` 550→ 551→Use it when you need to refresh the client router after performing an action. 552→ 553→### cacheLife and cacheTag 554→ 555→[`cacheLife`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/cacheLife) and [`cacheTag`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/cacheTag) are now stable. The `unstable_` prefix is no longer needed. 556→ 557→Wherever you had aliased imports like: 558→ 559→```ts 560→import { 561→ unstable_cacheLife as cacheLife, 562→ unstable_cacheTag as cacheTag, 563→} from 'next/cache' 564→``` 565→ 566→You can update your imports to: 567→ 568→```ts 569→import { cacheLife, cacheTag } from 'next/cache' 570→``` 571→ 572→## Enhanced Routing and Navigation 573→ 574→**Next.js 16** includes a complete overhaul of the routing and navigation system, making page transitions leaner and faster. This optimizes how Next.js prefetches and caches navigation data: 575→ 576→- **Layout deduplication**: When prefetching multiple URLs with a shared layout, the layout is downloaded once. 577→- **Incremental prefetching**: Next.js only prefetches parts not already in cache, rather than entire pages. 578→ 579→These changes require **no code modifications** and are designed to improve performance across all apps. 580→ 581→However, you may see more individual prefetch requests with much lower total transfer sizes. We believe this is the right trade-off for nearly all applications. 582→ 583→If the increased request count causes issues, please let us know by creating an [issue](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/issues) or [discussion](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/discussions) item. 584→ 585→## Partial Prerendering (PPR) 586→ 587→**Next.js 16** removes the experimental **Partial Prerendering (PPR)** flag and configuration options, including the route level segment `experimental_ppr`. 588→ 589→Starting with **Next.js 16**, you can opt into PPR using the [`cacheComponents`](/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/cacheComponents) configuration. 590→ 591→```js filename="next.config.js" 592→/** @type {import('next').NextConfig} */ 593→const nextConfig = { 594→ cacheComponents: true, 595→} 596→ 597→module.exports = nextConfig 598→``` 599→ 600→PPR in **Next.js 16** works differently than in **Next.js 15** canaries. If you are using PPR today, stay in the current Next.js 15 canary you are using. See [Migrating to Cache Components](/docs/app/guides/migrating-to-cache-components) for migration patterns. 601→ 602→```js filename="next.config.js" 603→/** @type {import('next').NextConfig} */ 604→const nextConfig = { 605→ // If you are using PPR today 606→ // stay in the current Next.js 15 canary 607→ experimental: { 608→ ppr: true, 609→ }, 610→} 611→ 612→module.exports = nextConfig 613→``` 614→ 615→## `middleware` to `proxy` 616→ 617→The `middleware` filename is deprecated, and has been renamed to `proxy` to clarify network boundary and routing focus. 618→ 619→The `edge` runtime is **NOT** supported in `proxy`. The `proxy` runtime is `nodejs`, and it cannot be configured. If you want to continue using the `edge` runtime, keep using `middleware`. We will follow up on a minor release with further `edge` runtime instructions. 620→ 621→```bash filename="Terminal" 622→# Rename your middleware file 623→mv middleware.ts proxy.ts 624→# or 625→mv middleware.js proxy.js 626→``` 627→ 628→The named export `middleware` is also deprecated. Rename your function to `proxy`. 629→ 630→```ts filename="proxy.ts" switcher 631→export function proxy(request: Request) {} 632→``` 633→ 634→```js filename="proxy.js" switcher 635→export function proxy(request) {} 636→``` 637→ 638→We recommend changing the function name to `proxy`, even if you are using a default export. 639→ 640→Configuration flags that contained the `middleware` name are also renamed. For example, `skipMiddlewareUrlNormalize` is now `skipProxyUrlNormalize` 641→ 642→```ts filename="next.config.ts" switcher 643→import type { NextConfig } from 'next' 644→ 645→const nextConfig: NextConfig = { 646→ skipProxyUrlNormalize: true, 647→} 648→ 649→export default nextConfig 650→``` 651→ 652→```js filename="next.config.js" switcher 653→/** @type {import('next').NextConfig} */ 654→const nextConfig = { 655→ skipProxyUrlNormalize: true, 656→} 657→ 658→module.exports = nextConfig 659→``` 660→ 661→The version 16 [codemod](/docs/app/guides/upgrading/codemods#160) is able to update these flags too. 662→ 663→## `next/image` changes 664→ 665→### Local Images with Query Strings (Breaking change) 666→ 667→Local image sources with query strings now require `images.localPatterns.search` configuration to prevent enumeration attacks. 668→ 669→```tsx filename="app/page.tsx" 670→import Image from 'next/image' 671→ 672→export default function Page() { 673→ return 674→} 675→``` 676→ 677→If you need to use query strings with local images, add the pattern to your configuration: 678→ 679→```ts filename="next.config.ts" switcher 680→import type { NextConfig } from 'next' 681→ 682→const nextConfig: NextConfig = { 683→ images: { 684→ localPatterns: [ 685→ { 686→ pathname: '/assets/**', 687→ search: '?v=1', 688→ }, 689→ ], 690→ }, 691→} 692→ 693→export default nextConfig 694→``` 695→ 696→```js filename="next.config.js" switcher 697→/** @type {import('next').NextConfig} */ 698→const nextConfig = { 699→ images: { 700→ localPatterns: [ 701→ { 702→ pathname: '/assets/**', 703→ search: '?v=1', 704→ }, 705→ ], 706→ }, 707→} 708→ 709→module.exports = nextConfig 710→``` 711→ 712→### `minimumCacheTTL` Default (Breaking change) 713→ 714→The default value for `images.minimumCacheTTL` has changed from `60 seconds` to `4 hours` (14400 seconds). This reduces revalidation cost for images without cache-control headers. 715→ 716→For some Next.js users, image revalidation was happening frequently, often because the upstream source images missed a `cache-control` header. This caused revalidation to happen every `60` seconds, which increased CPU usage and cost. 717→ 718→Since most images do not change often, this short interval is not ideal. Setting the default to 4 hours offers a more durable cache by default, while still allowing images to update a few times per day if needed. 719→ 720→If you need the previous behavior, change `minimumCacheTTL` to a lower value, for example back to `60` seconds: 721→ 722→```ts filename="next.config.ts" switcher 723→import type { NextConfig } from 'next' 724→ 725→const nextConfig: NextConfig = { 726→ images: { 727→ minimumCacheTTL: 60, 728→ }, 729→} 730→ 731→export default nextConfig 732→``` 733→ 734→```js filename="next.config.js" switcher 735→/** @type {import('next').NextConfig} */ 736→const nextConfig = { 737→ images: { 738→ minimumCacheTTL: 60, 739→ }, 740→} 741→ 742→module.exports = nextConfig 743→``` 744→ 745→### `imageSizes` Default (Breaking change) 746→ 747→The value `16` has been removed from the default `images.imageSizes` array. 748→ 749→We have looked at request analytics and found out that very few projects ever serve 16 pixels width images. Removing this setting reduces the size of the `srcset` attribute shipped to the browser by `next/image`. 750→ 751→If you need to support 16px images: 752→ 753→```ts filename="next.config.ts" switcher 754→import type { NextConfig } from 'next' 755→ 756→const nextConfig: NextConfig = { 757→ images: { 758→ imageSizes: [16, 32, 48, 64, 96, 128, 256, 384], 759→ }, 760→} 761→ 762→export default nextConfig 763→``` 764→ 765→```js filename="next.config.js" switcher 766→/** @type {import('next').NextConfig} */ 767→const nextConfig = { 768→ images: { 769→ imageSizes: [16, 32, 48, 64, 96, 128, 256, 384], 770→ }, 771→} 772→ 773→module.exports = nextConfig 774→``` 775→ 776→Rather than lack of developer usage, we believe 16 pixels width images have become less common, because `devicePixelRatio: 2` actually fetches a 32px image to prevent blurriness in retina displays. 777→ 778→### `qualities` Default (Breaking change) 779→ 780→The default value for `images.qualities` has changed from allowing all qualities to only `[75]`. 781→ 782→If you need to support multiple quality levels: 783→ 784→```ts filename="next.config.ts" switcher 785→import type { NextConfig } from 'next' 786→ 787→const nextConfig: NextConfig = { 788→ images: { 789→ qualities: [50, 75, 100], 790→ }, 791→} 792→ 793→export default nextConfig 794→``` 795→ 796→```js filename="next.config.js" switcher 797→/** @type {import('next').NextConfig} */ 798→const nextConfig = { 799→ images: { 800→ qualities: [50, 75, 100], 801→ }, 802→} 803→ 804→module.exports = nextConfig 805→``` 806→ 807→If you specify a `quality` prop not included in the `image.qualities` array, the quality will be coerced to the closest value in `images.qualities`. For example, given the configuration above, a `quality` prop of 80, is coerced to 75. 808→ 809→### Local IP Restriction (Breaking change) 810→ 811→A new security restriction blocks local IP optimization by default. Set `images.dangerouslyAllowLocalIP` to `true` only for private networks. 812→ 813→```ts filename="next.config.ts" switcher 814→import type { NextConfig } from 'next' 815→ 816→const nextConfig: NextConfig = { 817→ images: { 818→ dangerouslyAllowLocalIP: true, // Only for private networks 819→ }, 820→} 821→ 822→export default nextConfig 823→``` 824→ 825→```js filename="next.config.js" switcher 826→/** @type {import('next').NextConfig} */ 827→const nextConfig = { 828→ images: { 829→ dangerouslyAllowLocalIP: true, // Only for private networks 830→ }, 831→} 832→ 833→module.exports = nextConfig 834→``` 835→ 836→### Maximum Redirects (Breaking change) 837→ 838→The default for `images.maximumRedirects` has changed from unlimited to 3 redirects maximum. 839→ 840→```ts filename="next.config.ts" switcher 841→import type { NextConfig } from 'next' 842→ 843→const nextConfig: NextConfig = { 844→ images: { 845→ maximumRedirects: 0, // Disable redirects 846→ // or 847→ maximumRedirects: 5, // Increase for edge cases 848→ }, 849→} 850→ 851→export default nextConfig 852→``` 853→ 854→```js filename="next.config.js" switcher 855→/** @type {import('next').NextConfig} */ 856→const nextConfig = { 857→ images: { 858→ maximumRedirects: 0, // Disable redirects 859→ // or 860→ maximumRedirects: 5, // Increase for edge cases 861→ }, 862→} 863→ 864→module.exports = nextConfig 865→``` 866→ 867→### `next/legacy/image` Component (deprecated) 868→ 869→The `next/legacy/image` component is deprecated. Use `next/image` instead: 870→ 871→```tsx 872→// Before 873→import Image from 'next/legacy/image' 874→ 875→// After 876→import Image from 'next/image' 877→``` 878→ 879→### `images.domains` Configuration (deprecated) 880→ 881→The `images.domains` config is deprecated. 882→ 883→```js filename="next.config.js" 884→// image.domains is deprecated 885→module.exports = { 886→ images: { 887→ domains: ['example.com'], 888→ }, 889→} 890→``` 891→ 892→Use `images.remotePatterns` instead for improved security: 893→ 894→```js filename="next.config.js" 895→// Use image.remotePatterns instead 896→module.exports = { 897→ images: { 898→ remotePatterns: [ 899→ { 900→ protocol: 'https', 901→ hostname: 'example.com', 902→ }, 903→ ], 904→ }, 905→} 906→``` 907→ 908→## Concurrent `dev` and `build` 909→ 910→`next dev` and `next build` now use separate output directories, enabling concurrent execution. The `next dev` command outputs to `.next/dev`. 911→ 912→Additionally, a lockfile mechanism prevents multiple `next dev` or `next build` instances on the same project. 913→ 914→Since the development server outputs to `.next/dev`, the [Turbopack tracing command](/docs/app/guides/local-development#turbopack-tracing) should be: 915→ 916→```bash package="pnpm" 917→pnpm next internal trace .next/dev/trace-turbopack 918→``` 919→ 920→```bash package="npm" 921→npx next internal trace .next/dev/trace-turbopack 922→``` 923→ 924→```bash package="yarn" 925→yarn next internal trace .next/dev/trace-turbopack 926→``` 927→ 928→```bash package="bun" 929→bunx next internal trace .next/dev/trace-turbopack 930→``` 931→ 932→## Parallel Routes `default.js` requirement 933→ 934→All [parallel route](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/parallel-routes) slots now require explicit `default.js` files. Builds will fail without them. 935→ 936→To maintain previous behavior, create a [`default.js`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/default) file that calls `notFound()` or returns `null`. 937→ 938→```tsx filename="app/@modal/default.tsx" 939→import { notFound } from 'next/navigation' 940→ 941→export default function Default() { 942→ notFound() 943→} 944→``` 945→ 946→Or return `null`: 947→ 948→```tsx filename="app/@modal/default.tsx" 949→export default function Default() { 950→ return null 951→} 952→``` 953→ 954→## ESLint Flat Config 955→ 956→`@next/eslint-plugin-next` now defaults to ESLint Flat Config format, aligning with ESLint v10 which will drop legacy config support. 957→ 958→Make sure to review our API reference for the [`@next/eslint-plugin-next`](/docs/app/api-reference/config/eslint#setup-eslint) plugin. 959→ 960→If you're using the legacy `.eslintrc` format, consider migrating to the flat config format. See the [ESLint migration guide](https://eslint.org/docs/latest/use/configure/migration-guide) for details. 961→ 962→## Scroll Behavior Override 963→ 964→In **previous versions of Next.js**, if you had set `scroll-behavior: smooth` globally on your `` element via CSS, Next.js would override this during SPA route transitions, as follows: 965→ 966→1. Temporarily set `scroll-behavior` to `auto` 967→2. Perform the navigation (causing instant scroll to top) 968→3. Restore your original `scroll-behavior` value 969→ 970→This ensured that page navigation always felt snappy and instant, even when you had smooth scrolling enabled for in-page navigation. However, this manipulation could be expensive, especially at the start of every navigation. 971→ 972→In **Next.js 16**, this behavior has changed. By default, Next.js will **no longer override** your `scroll-behavior` setting during navigation. 973→ 974→**If you want Next.js to perform this override** (the previous default behavior), add the `data-scroll-behavior="smooth"` attribute to your `` element: 975→ 976→```tsx filename="app/layout.tsx" 977→export default function RootLayout({ children }) { 978→ return ( 979→ 980→ {children} 981→ 982→ ) 983→} 984→``` 985→ 986→## Performance Improvements 987→ 988→Significant performance optimizations for `next dev` and `next start` commands, along with improved terminal output with clearer formatting, better error messages, and improved performance metrics. 989→ 990→**Next.js 16** removes the `size` and `First Load JS` metrics from the `next build` output. We found these to be inaccurate in server-driven architectures using React Server Components. Both our Turbopack and Webpack implementations had issues, and disagreed on how to account for Client Components payload. 991→ 992→The most effective way to measure actual route performance is through tools such as [Chrome Lighthouse](https://developer.chrome.com/docs/lighthouse/overview) or Vercel Analytics, which focus on Core Web Vitals and downloaded resource sizes. 993→ 994→### `next dev` config load 995→ 996→In previous versions the Next config file was loaded twice during development: 997→ 998→- When running the `next dev` command 999→- When the `next dev` command started the Next.js server 1000→ 1001→This was inefficient because the `next dev` command doesn't need the config file to start the Next.js server. 1002→ 1003→A consequence of this change is that, when running `next dev` checking if `process.argv` includes `'dev'`, in your Next.js config file, will return `false`. 1004→ 1005→> **Good to know**: The `typegen`, and `build` commands, are still visible in `process.argv`. 1006→ 1007→This is specially important for plugins that trigger side-effects on `next dev`. If that's the case, it might be enough to check if `NODE_ENV` is set to `development`. 1008→ 1009→```js filename="next.config.js" 1010→import { startServer } from 'docs-lib/dev-server' 1011→ 1012→const isDev = process.env.NODE_ENV === 'development' 1013→ 1014→if (isDev) { 1015→ startServer() 1016→} 1017→ 1018→const nextConfig = { 1019→ /* Your config options */ 1020→} 1021→ 1022→module.exports = nextConfig 1023→``` 1024→ 1025→Alternatively, use the [`phase`](/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js#phase) in which the configuration is loaded. 1026→ 1027→## Build Adapters API (alpha) 1028→ 1029→Following the [Build Adapters RFC](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/discussions/77740), the first alpha version of the Build Adapters API is now available. 1030→ 1031→Build Adapters allow you to create custom adapters that hook into the build process, enabling deployment platforms and custom build integrations to modify Next.js configuration or process build output. 1032→ 1033→```js filename="next.config.js" 1034→const nextConfig = { 1035→ experimental: { 1036→ adapterPath: require.resolve('./my-adapter.js'), 1037→ }, 1038→} 1039→ 1040→module.exports = nextConfig 1041→``` 1042→ 1043→`adapterPath` was promoted to a stable, top-level option in 16.2.0. See [`adapterPath`](/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/adapterPath) for the current API reference. 1044→ 1045→## Modern Sass API 1046→ 1047→`sass-loader` has been bumped to v16, which supports [modern Sass syntax](https://sass-lang.com/documentation/js-api/#md:usage) and new features. 1048→ 1049→## Removals 1050→ 1051→These features were previously deprecated and are now removed: 1052→ 1053→### AMP Support 1054→ 1055→AMP adoption has declined significantly, and maintaining this feature adds complexity to the framework. All AMP APIs and configurations have been removed: 1056→ 1057→- `amp` configuration from your Next config file 1058→- `next/amp` hook imports and usage (`useAmp`) 1059→ 1060→```tsx 1061→// Removed 1062→import { useAmp } from 'next/amp' 1063→ 1064→// Removed 1065→export const config = { amp: true } 1066→``` 1067→ 1068→- `export const config = { amp: true }` from pages 1069→ 1070→```js filename="next.config.js" 1071→const nextConfig = { 1072→ // Removed 1073→ amp: { 1074→ canonicalBase: 'https://example.com', 1075→ }, 1076→} 1077→ 1078→export default nextConfig 1079→``` 1080→ 1081→Evaluate if AMP is still necessary for your use case. Most performance benefits can now be achieved through Next.js's built-in optimizations and modern web standards. 1082→ 1083→### `next lint` Command 1084→ 1085→The `next lint` command has been removed. input.content:
# ReviewLens AI — Review Intelligence Portal

A secure, web-based portal that ingests product reviews from Amazon and enables analysts to interrogate that data through a **guardrailed AI Q&A interface** — without the AI ever drifting off-topic.

**Live URL:** _[deployed URL here]_

---

## Architecture

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    Next.js 16 (App Router)                   │
│                    Deployed on AWS Amplify                    │
│                                                              │
│   ┌───────────┐   ┌──────────────┐   ┌──────────────────┐   │
│   │  Ingest    │   │  Dashboard    │   │  Q&A Chat        │   │
│   │  URL+CSV   │   │  Stats+Charts │   │  Guardrailed     │   │
│   └─────┬─────┘   └──────┬───────┘   └────────┬─────────┘   │
│         │                │                     │              │
│   ┌─────▼────────────────▼─────────────────────▼──────────┐  │
│   │                Next.js API Routes                      │  │
│   │  POST /api/ingest  — scrape or parse CSV               │  │
│   │  POST /api/chat    — guardrailed Q&A (SSE streaming)   │  │
│   │  GET  /api/reviews — retrieve stored reviews           │  │
│   │  GET  /api/analytics — sentiment + stats               │  │
│   └───┬──────────────────────────────┬────────────────────┘  │
│       │                              │                       │
│  ┌────▼──────────┐    ┌──────────────▼──────────────────┐    │
│  │ Scraper        │    │ Amazon Bedrock                   │    │
│  │ Apify (primary)│    │ ┌──────────────────────────┐    │    │
│  │ Lambda (backup)│    │ │ Bedrock Guardrails (L1)  │    │    │
│  │ CSV (fallback) │    │ │ Denied topic policies    │    │    │
│  └────────────────┘    │ └───────────┬──────────────┘    │    │
│                        │ ┌───────────▼──────────────┐    │    │
│  ┌────────────────┐    │ │ Claude 3.5 Haiku (L2)   │    │    │
│  │ AWS Comprehend │    │ │ System prompt guard     │    │    │
│  │ (Sentiment)    │    │ └──────────────────────────┘    │    │
│  └────────────────┘    └─────────────────────────────────┘    │
│                                                              │
│  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐    │
│  │  In-Memory Store (Map<sessionId, ReviewSession>)     │    │
│  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘    │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

## Tech Stack

| Layer | Technology |
|-------|-----------|
| Framework | Next.js 16.2 (App Router, TypeScript, Turbopack) |
| Styling | Tailwind CSS v4 + shadcn/ui |
| LLM | Amazon Bedrock — Claude 3.5 Haiku |
| Scope Guard L1 | Amazon Bedrock Guardrails (denied topic policies) |
| Scope Guard L2 | System prompt engineering |
| Sentiment Analysis | Amazon Comprehend |
| Scraping (primary) | Apify — Amazon Reviews Scraper |
| Scraping (backup) | AWS Lambda + Puppeteer + @sparticuz/chromium |
| Import (fallback) | CSV upload / paste |
| Charts | Recharts |
| Storage | In-memory (prototype) |
| Deployment | AWS Amplify |

## Key Design Decision: Dual-Layer Scope Guard

The assignment emphasizes scope guard enforcement. Rather than relying solely on a system prompt, I implemented a **dual-layer approach**:

### Layer 1 — Amazon Bedrock Guardrails (Infrastructure-Level)
- Configured via AWS with **denied topic policies**: weather, news, politics, sports, other review platforms, general knowledge, competitor comparisons
- Applied to every Bedrock API call via `guardrailConfig`
- Blocks off-topic queries **before the LLM even processes them**
- Returns a structured intervention that the UI displays distinctly

### Layer 2 — System Prompt Engineering (Application-Level)
- Strict scoping: "You ONLY answer about the provided reviews"
- Citation requirement: responses must reference specific review numbers
- Graceful decline template for edge cases that slip past the guardrail
- Dynamic context injection: platform, product name, review count, date range

This is how you'd build scope enforcement in production — infrastructure-level guardrails for hard boundaries, prompt engineering for nuance and UX.

## Scraping Strategy

Three-tier fallback for reliability:

1. **Apify** (primary) — Pre-built Amazon scraper, handles anti-bot, pagination, proxy rotation. ~60s for 100 reviews.
2. **AWS Lambda + Puppeteer** (backup) — Headless Chromium via `@sparticuz/chromium`. In the repo under `/lambda/`.
3. **CSV Upload** (always works) — User pastes or uploads review data directly. Supports flexible column names.

## Getting Started

### Prerequisites
- Node.js 20+
- AWS account with Bedrock access (Claude 3.5 Haiku enabled)
- Apify account (free tier: 5 USD/month compute)

### Setup

```bash
# Clone the repo
git clone https://github.com/YOUR_USERNAME/reviewlens-ai.git
cd reviewlens-ai

# Install dependencies
npm install

# Copy environment template
cp .env.example .env.local
```

### Configure Environment Variables

```env
# AWS
AWS_REGION=us-east-1
AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID=your_key
AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY=your_secret

# Bedrock
BEDROCK_MODEL_ID=anthropic.claude-3-5-haiku-20241022-v1:0
BEDROCK_GUARDRAIL_ID=your_guardrail_id
BEDROCK_GUARDRAIL_VERSION=DRAFT

# Apify
APIFY_API_TOKEN=your_apify_token

# Lambda (optional)
SCRAPER_LAMBDA_URL=https://your-lambda-url.on.aws
```

### Create Bedrock Guardrail

```bash
# Automated setup script
chmod +x scripts/create-guardrail.sh
./scripts/create-guardrail.sh
```

### Run Locally

```bash
npm run dev
# Open http://localhost:3000
```

### Deploy Lambda Scraper (Optional)

```bash
chmod +x scripts/deploy-lambda.sh
LAMBDA_ROLE_ARN=arn:aws:iam::ACCOUNT:role/YOUR_ROLE ./scripts/deploy-lambda.sh
```

## Assumptions

1. **Amazon as target platform** — Most structured review data, well-supported by scraping tools
2. **In-memory storage** — Acceptable for a prototype; production would use DynamoDB
3. **No user authentication** — Per assignment requirements, the app is directly accessible
4. **Bedrock costs** — Claude 3.5 Haiku is ~$0.001/query; total demo cost < $0.50
5. **Apify free tier** — 5 USD/month compute credit is sufficient for demo and evaluation
6. **English reviews only** — Comprehend sentiment analysis configured for English

## What I'd Do With More Time

- **DynamoDB persistence** — Sessions survive server restarts
- **Multi-product comparison** — Compare sentiment across products
- **PDF/CSV export** — Download analysis reports
- **WebSocket real-time** — True real-time streaming via WebSockets
- **Rate limiting** — Protect the Bedrock API from abuse
- **Review pagination** — Handle products with 10,000+ reviews
- **Dark mode toggle** — Already have CSS variables set up for it
- **E2E tests** — Playwright tests for the full flow

---

Built with Next.js 16, Amazon Bedrock, Bedrock Guardrails, Amazon Comprehend, Apify, and Claude Code.

This is a [Next.js](https://nextjs.org) project bootstrapped with [`create-next-app`](https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/cli/create-next-app).

## Getting Started

First, run the development server:

```bash
npm run dev
# or
yarn dev
# or
pnpm dev
# or
bun dev
```

Open [http://localhost:3000](http://localhost:3000) with your browser to see the result.

You can start editing the page by modifying `app/page.tsx`. The page auto-updates as you edit the file.

This project uses [`next/font`](https://nextjs.org/docs/app/building-your-application/optimizing/fonts) to automatically optimize and load [Geist](https://vercel.com/font), a new font family for Vercel.

## Learn More

To learn more about Next.js, take a look at the following resources:

- [Next.js Documentation](https://nextjs.org/docs) - learn about Next.js features and API.
- [Learn Next.js](https://nextjs.org/learn) - an interactive Next.js tutorial.

You can check out [the Next.js GitHub repository](https://github.com/vercel/next.js) - your feedback and contributions are welcome!

## Deploy on Vercel

The easiest way to deploy your Next.js app is to use the [Vercel Platform](https://vercel.com/new?utm_medium=default-template&filter=next.js&utm_source=create-next-app&utm_campaign=create-next-app-readme) from the creators of Next.js.

## Tech Stack

| Layer | Technology |
|-------|-----------|
| Framework | Next.js 16.2 (App Router, TypeScript, Turbopack) |
| Styling | Tailwind CSS v4 + shadcn/ui |
| LLM | Amazon Bedrock — Claude 3.5 Haiku |
| Scope Guard L1 | Amazon Bedrock Guardrails (denied topic policies) |
| Scope Guard L2 | System prompt engineering |
| Sentiment Analysis | Amazon Comprehend |
| Scraping (primary) | Apify — Amazon Reviews Scraper |
| Scraping (backup) | AWS Lambda + Puppeteer + @sparticuz/chromium |
| Import (fallback) | CSV upload / paste |
| Charts | Recharts |
| Storage | In-memory (prototype) |
| Deployment | AWS Amplify |

## Key Design Decision: Dual-Layer Scope Guard

The assignment emphasizes scope guard enforcement. Rather than relying solely on a system prompt, I implemented a **dual-layer approach**:

### Layer 1 — Amazon Bedrock Guardrails (Infrastructure-Level)
- Configured via AWS with **denied topic policies**: weather, news, politics, sports, other review platforms, general knowledge, competitor comparisons
- Applied to every Bedrock API call via `guardrailConfig`
- Blocks off-topic queries **before the LLM even processes them**
- Returns a structured intervention that the UI displays distinctly

### Layer 2 — System Prompt Engineering (Application-Level)
- Strict scoping: "You ONLY answer about the provided reviews"
- Citation requirement: responses must reference specific review numbers
- Graceful decline template for edge cases that slip past the guardrail
- Dynamic context injection: platform, product name, review count, date range

This is how you'd build scope enforcement in production — infrastructure-level guardrails for hard boundaries, prompt engineering for nuance and UX.

## Scraping Strategy

Three-tier fallback for reliability:

1. **Apify** (primary) — Pre-built Amazon scraper, handles anti-bot, pagination, proxy rotation. ~60s for 100 reviews.
2. **AWS Lambda + Puppeteer** (backup) — Headless Chromium via `@sparticuz/chromium`. In the repo under `/lambda/`.
3. **CSV Upload** (always works) — User pastes or uploads review data directly. Supports flexible column names.

## Getting Started

### Prerequisites
- Node.js 20+
- AWS account with Bedrock access (Claude 3.5 Haiku enabled)
- Apify account (free tier: 5 USD/month compute)

### Setup

```bash
# Clone the repo
git clone https://github.com/YOUR_USERNAME/reviewlens-ai.git
cd reviewlens-ai

# Install dependencies
npm install

# Copy environment template
cp .env.example .env.local
```

### Configure Environment Variables

```env
# AWS
AWS_REGION=us-east-1
AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID=your_key
AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY=your_secret

# Bedrock
BEDROCK_MODEL_ID=anthropic.claude-3-5-haiku-20241022-v1:0
BEDROCK_GUARDRAIL_ID=your_guardrail_id
BEDROCK_GUARDRAIL_VERSION=DRAFT

# Apify
APIFY_API_TOKEN=your_apify_token

# Lambda (optional)
SCRAPER_LAMBDA_URL=https://your-lambda-url.on.aws
```

### Create Bedrock Guardrail

```bash
# Automated setup script
chmod +x scripts/create-guardrail.sh
./scripts/create-guardrail.sh
```

### Run Locally

```bash
npm run dev
# Open http://localhost:3000
```

### Deploy Lambda Scraper (Optional)

```bash
chmod +x scripts/deploy-lambda.sh
LAMBDA_ROLE_ARN=arn:aws:iam::ACCOUNT:role/YOUR_ROLE ./scripts/deploy-lambda.sh
```

## Assumptions

1. **Amazon as target platform** — Most structured review data, well-supported by scraping tools
2. **In-memory storage** — Acceptable for a prototype; production would use DynamoDB
3. **No user authentication** — Per assignment requirements, the app is directly accessible
4. **Bedrock costs** — Claude 3.5 Haiku is ~$0.001/query; total demo cost < $0.50
5. **Apify free tier** — 5 USD/month compute credit is sufficient for demo and evaluation
6. **English reviews only** — Comprehend sentiment analysis configured for English

## What I'd Do With More Time

- **DynamoDB persistence** — Sessions survive server restarts
- **Multi-product comparison** — Compare sentiment across products
- **PDF/CSV export** — Download analysis reports
- **WebSocket real-time** — True real-time streaming via WebSockets
- **Rate limiting** — Protect the Bedrock API from abuse
- **Review pagination** — Handle products with 10,000+ reviews
- **Dark mode toggle** — Already have CSS variables set up for it
- **E2E tests** — Playwright tests for the full flow

---

Built with Next.js 16, Amazon Bedrock, Bedrock Guardrails, Amazon Comprehend, Apify, and Claude Code.

# ReviewLens AI — Review Intelligence Portal

A secure, web-based portal that ingests product reviews from Amazon and enables analysts to interrogate that data through a **guardrailed AI Q&A interface** — without the AI ever drifting off-topic.

**Live URL:** _[deployed URL here]_

---

## Architecture

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    Next.js 16 (App Router)                   │
│                    Deployed on AWS Amplify                    │
│                                                              │
│   ┌───────────┐   ┌──────────────┐   ┌──────────────────┐   │
│   │  Ingest    │   │  Dashboard    │   │  Q&A Chat        │   │
│   │  URL+CSV   │   │  Stats+Charts │   │  Guardrailed     │   │
│   └─────┬─────┘   └──────┬───────┘   └────────┬─────────┘   │
│         │                │                     │              │
│   ┌─────▼────────────────▼─────────────────────▼──────────┐  │
│   │                Next.js API Routes                      │  │
│   │  POST /api/ingest  — scrape or parse CSV               │  │
│   │  POST /api/chat    — guardrailed Q&A (SSE streaming)   │  │
│   │  GET  /api/reviews — retrieve stored reviews           │  │
│   │  GET  /api/analytics — sentiment + stats               │  │
│   └───┬──────────────────────────────┬────────────────────┘  │
│       │                              │                       │
│  ┌────▼──────────┐    ┌──────────────▼──────────────────┐    │
│  │ Scraper        │    │ Amazon Bedrock                   │    │
│  │ Apify (primary)│    │ ┌──────────────────────────┐    │    │
│  │ Lambda (backup)│    │ │ Bedrock Guardrails (L1)  │    │    │
│  │ CSV (fallback) │    │ │ Denied topic policies    │    │    │
│  └────────────────┘    │ └───────────┬──────────────┘    │    │

│  ┌────────────────┐    │ │ Claude 3.5 Haiku (L2)   │    │    │

│  │ (Sentiment)    │    │ └──────────────────────────┘    │    │
│  └────────────────┘    └─────────────────────────────────┘    │
│                                                              │
│  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐    │

│  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘    │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

| Scraping (backup) | AWS Lambda + Puppeteer + @sparticuz/chromium |
| Import (fallback) | CSV upload / paste |

content.text:
Latest Claude models available:
- **Claude Sonnet 4.6** (`anthropic.claude-sonnet-4-6`) — latest & most capable
- **Claude Haiku 4.5** (`anthropic.claude-haiku-4-5-20251001-v1:0`) — latest fast/cheap
- **Claude Opus 4.6** (`anthropic.claude-opus-4-6-v1`) — most powerful

For this project, **Claude Haiku 4.5** is the best fit — it's the latest generation, very fast, cheap (~$0.001/query), and more than capable for review analysis. Sonnet 4.6 would work too but costs more.

content.text: Now let me create the Bedrock Guardrail in us-east-1 with the `project: reviewlens-ai` tag.

input.command:
aws bedrock create-guardrail \
  --region us-east-1 \
  --name "reviewlens-scope-guard" \
  --description "Scope guard for ReviewLens AI - blocks off-topic queries not related to ingested product reviews" \
  --blocked-input-messaging "I can only analyze the product reviews that have been loaded into this session. That question falls outside the scope of the ingested review data. Feel free to ask me anything about the reviews!" \
  --blocked-outputs-messaging "I can only provide analysis based on the ingested product reviews. I am unable to help with that topic. Please ask me something about the reviews!" \
  --topic-policy-config '{
    "topicsConfig": [
      {
        "name": "Weather",
        "definition": "Questions about weather, temperature, climate, forecasts, or meteorological conditions",
        "type": "DENY",
        "examples": [
          "What is the weather today?",
          "Will it rain tomorrow?",
          "What is the temperature in Toronto?"
        ]
      },
      {
        "name": "News_Politics_Sports",
        "definition": "Questions about current events, news, politics, elections, government, sports scores, games, or athletic events",
        "type": "DENY",
        "examples": [
          "Who won the election?",
          "What is the latest news?",
          "What was the score of the game?"
        ]
      },
      {
        "name": "Other_Review_Platforms",
        "definition": "Questions about reviews from platforms other than the one currently being analyzed, such as Google Maps reviews, Yelp reviews, G2 reviews, Trustpilot reviews, or any other review platform not currently loaded",
        "type": "DENY",
        "examples": [
          "What do Google reviews say about this?",
          "How are Yelp reviews for this product?",
          "What does G2 say about this?",
          "Compare Amazon reviews with Google reviews"
        ]
      },
      {
        "name": "General_Knowledge",
        "definition": "General knowledge questions, trivia, coding help, math problems, history, science, or any topic not directly about analyzing the specific product reviews loaded in this session",
        "type": "DENY",
        "examples": [
          "What is the capital of France?",
          "Help me write a Python script",
          "What is the meaning of life?",
          "Explain quantum physics",
          "How do I cook pasta?"
        ]
      },
      {
        "name": "Competitor_Comparisons",
        "definition": "Questions asking to compare the product with competitor products using knowledge outside the loaded reviews. Only comparisons explicitly mentioned within the reviews themselves are allowed",
        "type": "DENY",
        "examples": [
          "How does this compare to the Samsung version?",
          "Is the Apple alternative better?",
          "Which competitor product should I buy instead?"
        ]
      }
    ]
  }' \
  --content-policy-config '{
    "filtersConfig": [
      {"type": "HATE", "inputStrength": "HIGH", "outputStrength": "HIGH"},
      {"type": "INSULTS", "inputStrength": "HIGH", "outputStrength": "HIGH"},
      {"type": "SEXUAL", "inputStrength": "HIGH", "outputStrength": "HIGH"},
      {"type": "VIOLENCE", "inputStrength": "HIGH", "outputStrength": "HIGH"},
      {"type": "MISCONDUCT", "inputStrength": "HIGH", "outputStrength": "HIGH"},
      {"type": "PROMPT_ATTACK", "inputStrength": "HIGH", "outputStrength": "NONE"}
    ]
  }' \
  --tags '[{"key": "project", "value": "reviewlens-ai"}]' \
  2>&1

content.text:
Good question. Currently there's no dedicated "agent" — the chat is a direct **Bedrock Converse API call** with a system prompt and guardrails, handled in:

- [bedrock.ts](src/lib/bedrock.ts) — Bedrock client + streaming
- [prompts.ts](src/lib/prompts.ts) — System prompt builder
- [api/chat/route.ts](src/app/api/chat/route.ts) — Orchestration

Are you asking about building an actual **Amazon Bedrock Agent** for this? That would mean:

- A Bedrock Agent with a **Knowledge Base** (reviews loaded as the data source)
- **Guardrails attached** at the agent level
- **Action Groups** for analytics queries
- Agent handles conversation memory, citation, and scope enforcement natively

That's more architecturally impressive but adds setup complexity. For this prototype, the trade-offs are:

| Approach | Pros | Cons |
|----------|------|------|
| **Current** (Converse API + system prompt) | Simple, fast to build, works now | No built-in memory/RAG, reviews in prompt context |
| **Bedrock Agent + Knowledge Base** | Production-grade, native RAG, built-in citations, impressive architecture | More AWS setup, reviews need to be in S3 for KB ingestion |

Given your Bedrock Agents experience from LifeLine/SkinScan, do you want me to switch to a **Bedrock Agent architecture**? It would be a stronger differentiator but will take more setup time. What's your call?

content.thinking:
The user is asking my opinion on whether they need a Bedrock Agent for the chatbot functionality, and how they're achieving it without one.

Let me explain clearly: the current implementation IS a chatbot. It just doesn't use Bedrock Agents specifically. The chat works via:

1. User sends message
2. API route builds a system prompt with all review data injected
3. Calls Bedrock Converse API (with guardrails) with the full conversation history
4. Streams the response back

This is actually how most AI chatbots work — direct LLM calls with context. Bedrock Agents add orchestration, tool use, and RAG on top, but they're not required for a chatbot.


The direct Converse API approach is actually ideal for this use case since the reviews fit comfortably within the context window and there's no need for vector search or RAG. The only scenario where you'd need to reconsider is if you're dealing with thousands of reviews that exceed the token limit, but for 50-200 reviews, this simpler approach is faster and more efficient.

content.text:
Here's my honest take:

**What you have right now IS a chatbot.** It just doesn't use the Bedrock Agents service specifically. The current flow:

```
User message → API route → builds system prompt with reviews → 
Bedrock Converse API (with guardrails) → streams response back → 
client stores chat history → sends history with next message
```

This is how most production AI chatbots work — direct LLM calls with context injection and conversation history. ChatGPT itself works this way.

**Why Bedrock Agents would be overkill here:**

- Your reviews (~50-200) easily fit in Opus 4.6's context window (200K tokens). No need for RAG/Knowledge Base.
- The assignment says *"primarily driven by your system prompt configuration"* — they WANT to see your prompt engineering, not an abstracted agent service.
- Bedrock Agents add latency (agent orchestration overhead) and complexity without meaningful benefit for this review count.
- You'd lose visibility into the prompt engineering — the evaluators can't see what's happening inside a managed agent.

**When you WOULD need a Bedrock Agent:**
- 10,000+ reviews that don't fit in context → need Knowledge Base + RAG
- Multiple tools/actions (search reviews, generate reports, export data) → need Action Groups
- Complex multi-step reasoning → need agent orchestration

**Bottom line:** Your current approach is the right one for this assignment. It's a fully functional chatbot with streaming, conversation history, and dual-layer scope guard. A Bedrock Agent would actually hurt you here — it hides the prompt engineering that evaluators want to see.

Want to move forward with deployment, or is there anything else you'd like to adjust?

input.content:
# ReviewLens AI — Future Improvements

## Current Prototype Approach

For this prototype, ReviewLens AI uses **direct LLM invocation via Amazon Bedrock Converse API** with a carefully engineered system prompt and Bedrock Guardrails for scope enforcement. This approach is intentional:

- Reviews (~50-200) fit comfortably within Claude Opus 4.6's 200K token context window
- System prompt engineering is transparent and auditable — evaluators can inspect exactly how scope guarding works
- Minimal infrastructure overhead — no additional AWS services beyond Bedrock and Comprehend
- Conversation history is maintained client-side and sent with each request for multi-turn context
- Streaming responses via Bedrock ConverseStream API for real-time UX

This is sufficient and performant for the prototype's review volume. The following improvements would be necessary for a production-scale system.

---

## Phase 1: Amazon Bedrock Agent Architecture

### Why
When review volume exceeds the context window (~10,000+ reviews), injecting all reviews into the system prompt becomes impractical. A Bedrock Agent with a Knowledge Base enables retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) — the agent retrieves only the most relevant reviews for each query.

### Implementation
- **Bedrock Agent** — Orchestrates multi-step reasoning, maintains conversation state server-side, and manages tool invocations
- **Bedrock Knowledge Base** — Reviews stored in S3, indexed via OpenSearch Serverless vector store. Agent retrieves semantically relevant reviews per query instead of loading all reviews into context
- **Action Groups** — Lambda-backed actions for structured operations:
  - `SearchReviews` — Filter reviews by rating, date range, keyword
  - `GetAnalytics` — Return sentiment breakdown, rating distribution
  - `ExportReport` — Generate PDF/CSV analysis reports
  - `CompareTimePeriods` — Compare sentiment across date ranges
- **Guardrails** — Attached at the agent level (same guardrail, applied automatically to every invocation)

### Architecture
```
User Query
    │
    ▼
┌──────────────────────────────┐
│  Amazon Bedrock Agent         │
│  ┌────────────────────────┐  │
│  │ Guardrails (attached)  │  │
│  └───────────┬────────────┘  │
│              │               │
│  ┌───────────▼────────────┐  │
│  │ Knowledge Base (RAG)   │  │
│  │ S3 → OpenSearch Vector │  │
│  └───────────┬────────────┘  │
│              │               │
│  ┌───────────▼────────────┐  │
│  │ Action Groups          │  │
│  │ - SearchReviews        │  │
│  │ - GetAnalytics         │  │
│  │ - ExportReport         │  │
│  └────────────────────────┘  │
└──────────────────────────────┘
    │
    ▼
Streaming Response with Citations
```

### Benefits
- Handles 100,000+ reviews without context window limits
- Built-in citation from Knowledge Base retrieval
- Server-side conversation memory (no need to send full history each request)
- Native multi-step reasoning (agent can search → analyze → summarize in one turn)
- Automatic guardrail enforcement at agent level

---

## Phase 2: Data Persistence & Multi-Tenancy

### DynamoDB for Session Persistence
- Replace in-memory store with DynamoDB
- Sessions survive server restarts and support horizontal scaling
- TTL-based auto-expiry for sessions (e.g., 24-hour retention)
- Table design: `PK: sessionId`, `SK: reviewId` for efficient per-review access

### S3 for Review Storage
- Raw review data stored in S3 (input for Knowledge Base)
- Processed analytics cached as JSON in S3
- Pre-signed URLs for CSV/PDF export downloads

### Multi-Tenancy
- Separate Knowledge Base per product/entity for strict data isolation
- API Gateway with usage plans for rate limiting per client

---

## Phase 3: Advanced Analytics

### Real-Time Sentiment Tracking
- Amazon Kinesis Data Stream for continuous review ingestion
- Kinesis Data Firehose → S3 → Glue Crawler → Athena for trend queries
- CloudWatch dashboards for sentiment drift alerting

### Topic Modeling
- Amazon Comprehend custom entity recognition for product-specific themes
- Automatic topic clustering using Comprehend topic modeling API
- Visualization of topic evolution over time

### Comparative Analysis
- Multi-product comparison within the same platform
- Cross-period sentiment comparison (Q1 vs Q2, pre-launch vs post-launch)
- Automated insight generation: "Sentiment dropped 15% after firmware update on Jan 5"

---

## Phase 4: Production Hardening

### Security
- AWS Cognito for user authentication with RBAC
- API Gateway with WAF for DDoS protection
- VPC endpoints for Bedrock and Comprehend (no public internet)
- Secrets Manager for API keys (replace environment variables)

### Observability
- X-Ray tracing across Lambda, Bedrock, Comprehend calls
- CloudWatch Logs with structured JSON logging
- Custom CloudWatch metrics: latency per API, guardrail intervention rate, token usage
- Bedrock model invocation logging enabled for audit trail

### CI/CD
- AWS CDK infrastructure-as-code for all resources
- GitHub Actions pipeline: lint → test → build → deploy to Amplify
- Staging environment with separate Bedrock guardrail configuration
- Automated E2E tests with Playwright

### Cost Optimization
- Bedrock Provisioned Throughput for predictable latency at scale
- CloudFront caching for static dashboard data
- Lambda reserved concurrency for scraper to prevent cold starts
- S3 Intelligent-Tiering for archived review data

input.command:
git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
feat: complete ReviewLens AI — review intelligence portal

- Next.js 16 app with Tailwind CSS + shadcn/ui
- Amazon Bedrock (Claude Opus 4.6) for guardrailed Q&A with streaming
- Dual-layer scope guard: Bedrock Guardrails (L1) + system prompt (L2)
- Amazon Comprehend for sentiment analysis
- Three-tier scraping: Lambda/Puppeteer → Apify → CSV fallback
- Review dashboard with rating distribution, sentiment chart, keywords
- Chat interface with scope guard visual indicators
- In-memory session store for prototype

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)"

### CI/CD
- AWS CDK infrastructure-as-code for all resources
- GitHub Actions pipeline: lint → test → build → deploy to Amplify
- Staging environment with separate Bedrock guardrail configuration
- Automated E2E tests with Playwright

### Cost Optimization

- CloudFront caching for static dashboard data
- Lambda reserved concurrency for scraper to prevent cold starts
- S3 Intelligent-Tiering for archived review data

input.new_string:
### Network & Security Architecture

The current prototype deploys directly on AWS Amplify as a publicly accessible application — no VPC, no authentication, no API Gateway. This is intentional for a rapid prototype with zero-auth requirements.

In production, the architecture would be significantly hardened:

- **VPC with private subnets** — All compute (ECS/Lambda) runs in private subnets with no direct internet access
- **Application Load Balancer (ALB)** or **Network Load Balancer (NLB)** — ALB for HTTP/HTTPS traffic with SSL termination, path-based routing, and WAF integration. NLB if low-latency TCP/WebSocket connections are needed for real-time streaming
- **API Gateway** — RESTful API management with usage plans, throttling, request validation, and API key management. Acts as the single entry point for all backend services
- **Amazon Cognito** — User authentication with Amplify Auth integration. Supports MFA, social sign-in, and custom auth flows. RBAC via Cognito Groups (e.g., Analyst, Admin roles)
- **Amplify Auth** — Client-side auth UI components integrated with Cognito for seamless sign-up/sign-in flows
- **VPC Endpoints** — Private connectivity to Bedrock and Comprehend (no traffic over public internet)
- **AWS Secrets Manager** — All API keys and credentials stored securely (replace environment variables)
- **WAF (Web Application Firewall)** — Attached to ALB/API Gateway for SQL injection, XSS, and bot protection
